concise:
Ayah 1–32 · linked video: 01. Al-Furqan (Ayah 1-32)

Surah Al-Furqan: Commentary on Ayahs 1-32

Ayah 1

تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ نَذِيرًا

How blessed, how full of blessings and power to increase goodness is the One who sent down الفرقان – the Distinguisher, the Criterion – upon His slave. This opening establishes something extraordinary about the Quran: it is the criterion that makes a powerful separation between truth and falsehood, between good and evil.

The purpose is stated clearly: لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ نَذِيرًا – so that he may become a warner to all of the worlds. The Arabic structure here is atypical, emphasizing the universal scope of this message. Unlike previous messengers who were sent to specific nations, this messenger ﷺ is the first official global messenger. The phrase “for all worlds” carries profound implications – every nation, every civilization, every culture is included in this mission.

This raises an important question: the Prophet ﷺ did not personally engage with all nations. He barely ventured outside the Arabian Peninsula, sending only a few letters to neighboring rulers. How then can he be considered the warner to all nations? The answer lies in our responsibility. The light that started with him ﷺ must spread across the entire world. This title – warner to all nations – cannot be fulfilled until we do our job. He ﷺ started the mission and cultivated the nation that would carry this task forward.

When we encounter ayahs like this, they are not merely praise of the Prophet ﷺ. They are reminders of what this Ummah’s mission is. The warning has not reached the entire world yet. Every human being on the face of this earth should come into contact with a Muslim who shares the message of Islam with them. That responsibility stands on our shoulders. In Surah Al-Hajj, Allah ﷻ made us witnesses against humanity. The Messenger will testify against us, and we against humanity. This is the same concept expressed here.

Earlier in the Quran, in Surah Al-An’am, Allah ﷻ told the Prophet ﷺ: “The Quran has been revealed to me so I can warn you by means of it and whoever it reaches.” The message will reach other people one day through his followers. We are messengers of the Messenger ﷺ.

Ayah 2

ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ تَقْدِيرًا

This ayah describes the One who sent down the Furqan: He is the One who owns all dominion of the skies and the earth. This is very similar to the last ayah of Surah An-Nur. He didn’t take a son, and there is no associate with Him in any kind of dominion or kingdom.

The ayah establishes Allah’s absolute sovereignty through three declarations. First, He owns everything. Second, He has no offspring – a direct refutation of those who attributed sons or daughters to Him. Third, He has no partner in His rule. These aren’t philosophical statements; they are foundational truths about the nature of the Divine that directly counter the false beliefs of the polytheists.

Then comes the ultimate proof of His exclusive dominion: وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ – He created everything. Not only did He create everything, but فَقَدَّرَهُۥ تَقْدِيرًا – He calculated and planned everything out with precise calculation. The word تَقْدِير suggests meticulous planning, careful measurement, divine precision in every aspect of creation. Nothing exists haphazardly; everything has been designed with perfect wisdom.

Ayah 3

وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً لَّا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَوٰةً وَلَا نُشُورًا

After establishing Allah’s complete dominion, the ayah turns to expose the absurdity of what people have done: they’ve taken besides Him multiple gods. These false deities don’t create anything – rather, they themselves are created. Think about the irony: you worship something that had to be made, while ignoring the One who made everything.

The critique continues systematically. These idols don’t control for their own selves any harm or any benefit. When a storm happens or an earthquake strikes, these idols crack and break. They can’t even protect themselves. You have to put them in the shade. You have to protect them. If they can’t prevent harm from touching themselves, how can they help you?

The final blow: وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَوٰةً وَلَا نُشُورًا – they have no control or dominion over death or life, let alone bringing life from the dead. The word نُشُورًا specifically means resurrection – the dead rising from their graves and spreading forth. These false gods have no power over the most fundamental realities of existence, yet people turn to them for help.

Ayah 4

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ إِفْكٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ وَأَعَانَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ ءَاخَرُونَ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءُو ظُلْمًا وَزُورًا

The disbelievers came up with a new criticism. They said: “This is nothing but إِفْكٌ – a vicious lie.” إِفْك suggests not just any lie, but an evil, malicious fabrication. In modern terms borrowed from certain rhetoric, they were essentially saying: “Islam is an evil religion. It’s from the devil.”

Their accusation continues: ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ – he made it up. But they couldn’t even commit to this fully. They added: وَأَعَانَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ ءَاخَرُونَ – another group of people helped him. They tutor him on the side, they give him material. In other words, he’s got a lot of help when he comes up with this stuff, and he didn’t come up with it on his own.

Notice the contradiction: at least they admitted it can’t be from him alone. They’re acknowledging that there’s some other power behind it. But who are these people? They don’t know. When does he do it? He’s always preaching publicly. They don’t know. But there must be someone else giving him this material.

Allah ﷻ responds: فَقَدْ جَآءُو ظُلْمًا وَزُورًا – they have committed a huge crime. The word جَآءُو suggests they have brought forth or committed something. They’ve committed ظُلْمًا – a grossly wrong crime – and زُورًا, which means propaganda, or speech designed to turn people away from the truth. In modern terms, this is propaganda in its most malicious form.

Ayah 5

وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ ٱكْتَتَبَهَا فَهِىَ تُمْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا

Then they came up with another theory: “These are just أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ – stories of old times.” Why are people impressed with these stories? Don’t listen to that stuff. It has nothing new for you.

Their explanation continues: ٱكْتَتَبَهَا – he has them written down. He gets somebody to write these stories down for him. فَهِىَ تُمْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ – and they’re dictated onto him بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا – morning and night. It’s all he does: gets these stories dictated, written down, then comes and memorizes them, and shares them with everyone.

The interesting thing is they’re taking some elements of truth and mixing in falsehood. It is written down – by Allah ﷻ. It is dictated onto him – by Angel Jibreel (AS). And he does deliver it. They’ve taken the process and corrupted it just enough to make it seem false. This is a common propaganda technique: mix a little bit of falsehood with recognizable elements of truth to make the lie more believable.

Ayah 6

قُلْ أَنزَلَهُ ٱلَّذِى يَعْلَمُ ٱلسِّرَّ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ how to respond: قُلْ – tell them. The One who knows السِّرَّ – the secret – in the skies and the earth is the One who sent it down. The word السِّرَّ is singular but encompasses all secrets. The ultimate secret in the skies and the earth belongs to the One who revealed this Book.

He knows secrets you can’t even imagine. And He’s sharing some of those secrets with you – the secret to living a happy life, the secret of what comes after this world ends, the secret of your purpose. But you haven’t appreciated it.

Then comes a statement that seems surprising in this context: إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا – no doubt, He has always been extremely forgiving, always merciful. Why mention His mercy here? Because you people haven’t taken this message seriously all this time, and He hasn’t destroyed you. This is His mercy. You reject His Book, you mock His Messenger ﷺ, you fabricate lies against His revelation – and yet you still breathe, you still eat, you still enjoy His blessings. That itself is proof of His غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا nature.

Ayah 7

وَقَالُوا۟ مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشِى فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ ۙ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُۥ نَذِيرًا

Now they came up with yet another criticism: مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلرَّسُولِ – what’s wrong with this messenger? يَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّعَامَ – he eats food just like we do. I saw him eating lunch the other day. How could he be a messenger?

وَيَمْشِى فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ – and he walks in the markets. I watched him go shopping. This is a messenger? Really? That’s what messengers do – go shopping? This kind of talk reveals a particular kind of ignorance. When you encounter obnoxious statements like this, you don’t even know how to respond to this level of foolishness.

The reality is that people with low levels of intellect love this kind of talk. They get swayed by it. “He got him good! You speak the truth, brother! Tell it like it is!” And they won’t give the other side a chance to speak. Actually, the other side shouldn’t even speak – we’ll learn at the end of this surah that when the ignorant address the believers, they simply say “peace” and move on. There’s no point trying to reason with someone whose mind isn’t functioning properly. We have to know when not to engage.

Their complaints continue: لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ – how come an angel that comes to him with this Book doesn’t just keep hanging out with him so we can see the angel too? فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُۥ نَذِيرًا – the angel should warn alongside him, walking around where we can all see him. How come we don’t get to see this angel? You went shopping and had lunch, and we don’t get to see your angel companion. What’s so special about you?

Ayah 8

أَوْ يُلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَقَالَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا

They continue: أَوْ يُلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ – if you’re so special and this angel comes to you with this great treasure from the sky, how about some treasures from the earth? How come no treasure is thrown down to him? If you’re saying this came from the sky, well, there are lots of treasures in the sky. God has more. How come gold or silver or platinum isn’t coming down from the sky for you?

أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا – or how come gardens don’t just spring up for him in the middle of the desert that he could eat from? How come no divine food comes for him?

وَقَالَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ – and these wrongdoers end with this speech: إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا – you people are following nothing but somebody who is مَسْحُورًا. This word suggests someone who is possessed by magic, someone who is a victim, someone who’s been taken in by magic.

In modern terms, they’re saying: you’re following somebody who’s schizophrenic, somebody who’s psychotic, somebody who’s not in his right mind anymore. Somebody’s done a number on him. He’s traumatized. The psychological terms we use today would correspond to the old words مَجْنُون or مَسْحُور back then.

Ayah 9

ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ فَضَلُّوا۟ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا

Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ٱنظُرْ – observe. كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ – watch how they give examples for you, how they try to come up with case studies. Maybe this is the case with him, maybe that’s what’s going on. They’re trying to present theories about who you are, what you are.

Today in Islamic studies in the West, there are theses written about the Prophet ﷺ and his motivations. Papers are published about how he might have been schizophrenic and that’s why he came up with the Quran, or how he was an incredible plagiarizer, or how he was this or that. The same exact things, just in modern academic paper form – the same accusations that were said way back when. At least come up with something original.

One scholar, Montgomery Watt, came up with something different: his accusation was that the Prophet ﷺ never existed at all. The whole thing is an Arabic folk tale. Even his own academic peers laugh at this thesis to this day.

But here’s the problem: in academia, they say you have to come with an open mind and consider all possibilities. So when discussing the Quran, they start with premises like “when Muhammad wrote the Quran” or “when he made up the Quran.” If you object and say “I don’t accept the premise of the question – he didn’t write the Quran, he didn’t know how to write, and what he spoke wasn’t his, he delivered it,” they respond: “You have a faith bias.”

But they have a disbelief bias. Both have biases. They want to consider every possibility: he was schizophrenic, he plagiarized from Jews and Christians, he took stories from the Greeks, he came up with it on his own, or he never existed. Did you ever consider the possibility that he is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ? Is that in your “open-minded” list of possibilities? No, they say, faith is not open-minded enough for them. Faith is closed-minded.

That’s not actually open-minded. You go in with the premise that faith is not an option, that iman is not an option, that prophethood is not an option. You can only judge things from the seen; you can never say there’s anything in the unseen. If you can’t see it, you can’t talk about it reliably. If you’re going with that premise, of course you’re going to come up with flawed conclusions.

Muslim students study at these schools, study Middle Eastern Studies or Islamic Studies, and they come out confused. Why? Because these programs begin with the premise that there’s no such thing as the unseen. “Why are you worried about the unseen? You haven’t even seen it.” This is materialism taught as philosophy: only believe in what you can see as material reality. Don’t worry about anything else. Everything else is invalid.

This is actually a departure from all traditional societies, including Islamic but also other traditional philosophies. Every traditional society believed in some form of the unseen. Even philosophers like Buddha said things like “there is no greater reality than that which cannot be seen.” Pre-modern civilizations always acknowledged something beyond the material – whether a soul, a divine reality, something transcendent.

This idea was eliminated in the modern West. Why? Because believing in the unseen doesn’t make you money, doesn’t build factories, doesn’t build roads. You have to study science to build roads. We don’t deny science – but the shift was problematic. When Europeans talked about religion, when they talked about the unseen, they denied science. So the Western world developed an allergic reaction to religion itself. “What’s the point of studying religion? It held us back. It was the Dark Ages. We need light now. We need science.”

These academic theses will keep coming out. The undercurrent to all of them will be the same: we can’t even consider that this is revelation.

Allah ﷻ says: فَضَلُّوا۟ – they are so far lost. فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا – they’re not capable of finding a way back to the truth anymore. They are so bent upon coming up with some thesis that can prove you wrong, some explanation for this Quran other than the actual explanation, that they are no longer capable of actually considering the proper answer.

Ayah 10

تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِن شَآءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْرًا مِّن ذَٰلِكَ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ وَيَجْعَل لَّكَ قُصُورًۢا

تَبَارَكَ – how blessed is the One. This echoes the opening of the surah, but now with a different focus. They’re saying “How come he doesn’t have an angel walking around with him? How come there are no gardens he eats from?”

Allah ﷻ says: إِن شَآءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْرًا مِّن ذَٰلِكَ – if He wanted, He could have put way better stuff on this earth for you than what they’re suggesting. I could have given you what I’ve promised for the afterlife right here, right now. جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ – gardens beneath which rivers flow.

وَيَجْعَل لَّكَ قُصُورًۢا – and He would have made for you قُصُور. The word قُصُور comes from قَيْصَر – Caesar. These are castles, huge Roman-style castles and forts. Allah ﷻ is saying: I could have made the likes of Roman castles for you. You could have had those.

But the fact of the matter is – and this is the implied “but” that leads to the next ayah – these material things aren’t the point.

Ayah 11

بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِمَن كَذَّبَ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ سَعِيرًا

بَلْ – rather, the fact of the matter is: كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ – they have lied against the Hour, the Final Judgment. This is the real issue. All their objections about angels and gardens and treasures are distractions. The fundamental problem is they don’t believe in accountability.

وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِمَن كَذَّبَ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ سَعِيرًا – and whoever lies against the Final Hour, whoever denies it, We have prepared a blazing flame for him. The word سَعِير suggests an intensely burning fire.

Ayah 12

إِذَا رَأَتْهُم مِّن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا تَغَيُّظًا وَزَفِيرًا

Usually the Quran talks about when people see the Fire. This ayah is different: إِذَا رَأَتْهُم – when the Fire sees them. مِّن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ – from a distant place, when they’re still far away.

سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا تَغَيُّظًا – they will hear from it a تَغَيُّظًا – a piercing, ferocious roar. تَغَيُّظ is intense anger, rage. Imagine a lion in a cage that’s been kept hungry. The zookeepers bring meat from a distance. The lion starts making noise, starts roaring, gets aggressive because it’s about to attack that meat.

The Fire sees these people coming from a distance and starts roaring. Some scholars even say this isn’t only on the Day of Judgment – the Fire is already watching them right now. They’re walking toward it, getting closer to it, and it’s already starting to roar.

وَزَفِيرًا – and it will exhale. This is like the angry exhale an animal makes before it attacks. The Fire is described as a living entity that sees its victims approaching and responds with rage and anticipation.

Ayah 13

وَإِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مَكَانًا ضَيِّقًا مُّقَرَّنِينَ دَعَوْا۟ هُنَالِكَ ثُبُورًا

وَإِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ مِنْهَا – and when they are thrown into it. مَكَانًا ضَيِّقًا – into a tight corner of it. The word ضَيِّق suggests constriction, a narrow space where they’re compressed.

مُّقَرَّنِينَ – chained up, bound together. دَعَوْا۟ هُنَالِكَ ثُبُورًا – right then and there, the moment they’re thrown in, they call out: “Destroy us! Kill us now!”

ثُبُور in Arabic is used for violent death, for a wound that worsens until recovery is impossible. Like when you say someone is utterly destroyed, killed in a way where there’s no coming back. They’re begging: “Please, just let the torture end. Kill me now.”

The word هُنَالِكَ – right there at that moment – indicates this happens immediately. They haven’t even been tortured extensively yet. The very moment they’re thrown in, they beg for death.

Ayah 14

لَّا تَدْعُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ثُبُورًا وَٰحِدًا وَٱدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا كَثِيرًا

The response comes: لَّا تَدْعُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ثُبُورًا وَٰحِدًا – don’t call for destruction just once today. Don’t just ask for one death.

وَٱدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا كَثِيرًا – call out for many, many, many violent deaths. Call for destruction again and again and again. One death won’t be enough to express what you’re experiencing. The magnitude of the torment is such that a single death wouldn’t cover it. You’ll need to beg for death repeatedly, and still it won’t come.

Later in the surah, we’ll learn they haven’t even been thrown in yet when they start begging. And here, once inside, they beg again. The terror is so complete that death – which they used to fear – becomes their desperate plea.

Ayah 15

قُلْ أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۚ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزَآءً وَمَصِيرًا

قُلْ – tell them: أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ – is that better? أَمْ جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ – or the Gardens of Permanence?

جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ adds a dimension beyond just “they will be in it forever.” Usually خُلُود implies you remain forever. But جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ – Gardens of Permanence – implies the gardens themselves remain forever lush and green. They don’t have seasons where leaves fall or fruit stops growing. They’re always beautiful, always producing, permanently perfect.

ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ – which has been promised to those who protected themselves. The word مُتَّقُون always appears in particular contexts. The previous ayah talked about something terrifying. This ayah mentions those who protect themselves – first context would be protecting themselves from that Fire just described, that torture just detailed.

كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزَآءً وَمَصِيرًا – it’s going to be a compensation for them and a place they keep going back to. Both a reward and a final destination.

Ayah 16

لَّهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ خَـٰلِدِينَ ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ وَعْدًا مَّسْـُٔولًا

لَّهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ – they’re going to have in it whatever they want. خَـٰلِدِينَ – always, permanently. Not just that they remain forever, but that this state of having whatever they want continues without end.

كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ وَعْدًا مَّسْـُٔولًا – that is a promise mandated upon your Master, a promise that has been asked about. The word مَسْـُٔولًا is significant. People keep asking: “Ya Allah, didn’t You promise us Jannah? You promised us Jannah, right?”

Allah ﷻ says: it’s mandated on Me. مَسْـُٔولًا suggests it’s been asked about so much that it’s become obligatory. Not that Allah ﷻ has obligations in the way we do, but He’s made it binding upon Himself. When He makes a promise, He fulfills it. I’ll make sure I take care of you.

Ayah 17

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقُولُ ءَأَنتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبَادِى هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ – and the Day on which We will gather them. وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ – and whatever they used to worship other than Allah ﷻ.

The word مَا here is interesting. It’s an expression of anger against the things they worshipped. Sometimes they worshipped people, sometimes angels, sometimes righteous figures like ’Isa (AS). Allah ﷻ will bring all of them together along with their worshippers.

فَيَقُولُ – then He will say to those who were worshipped, the angels and righteous people: ءَأَنتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبَادِى هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ – did you misled My slaves? These slaves of Mine, did you lead them astray?

أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ – or was it the case that they themselves went off the path? They themselves went the wrong way?

Ayah 18

قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ مَا كَانَ يَنۢبَغِى لَنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ مِن دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِن مَّتَّعْتَهُمْ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَسُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا

قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ – they said: You are too perfect. سُبْحَـٰن expresses that Allah ﷻ is far above, too exalted for what’s being suggested. You’re too perfect for us to ever do something like that. You’re way above that kind of thing.

مَا كَانَ يَنۢبَغِى لَنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ مِن دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ – it was never appropriate for us that we should take for ourselves, besides You, any protective friends. We couldn’t even think of protective friends for ourselves except You. How could we offer ourselves as such to others?

Look at the rationale the righteous offer on the Day of Judgment. Then they explain what actually happened: وَلَـٰكِن مَّتَّعْتَهُمْ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ – but You, Ya Allah, You gave them so much in this world, and their ancestors too. You let them survive, You let them live generation after generation.

حَتَّىٰ نَسُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ – until they forgot the remembrance. When you have abundant enjoyment in life and it’s been passed down through generations – old money versus new money, let’s talk about old money – You provided for them and their ancestors. They had wealth and used that wealth to make more wealth. They were brought up in wealth and luxury, so many of them.

What does that do? It makes you feel like you don’t need anything. You have everything you need. And if you don’t need anything, you’re never going to look for the One who everyone needs. You’re never going to look for Him. These people didn’t feel the need for Allah ﷻ.

وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا – and they were a nation that was heading toward destruction. بُور actually means destruction of merchandise, or bankruptcy. Like somebody who runs a store, opens a business, and that business keeps going down year after year, but they’re holding on to it anyway, heading slowly toward inevitable bankruptcy.

There’s an interesting parallel here. In the same ayah, those righteous beings say “You gave them a lot,” describing these people as very rich. But then they describe them as a nation heading toward bankruptcy. They didn’t realize that every passing day, they were heading toward the kind of bankruptcy that can’t even be imagined. Material wealth, spiritual bankruptcy.

Ayah 19

فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُم بِمَا تَقُولُونَ فَمَا تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفًا وَلَا نَصْرًا ۚ وَمَن يَظْلِم مِّنكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذَابًا كَبِيرًا

Now Allah ﷻ turns to those criminals, those people who did shirk, and says: فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُم بِمَا تَقُولُونَ – well, they’ve lied against you, they’ve denied you. They’re saying you’re liars, they’re calling you liars.

This addresses the people who worshipped others besides Allah ﷻ. Those they worshipped – angels, righteous people – were just interrogated and they said they had nothing to do with this worship. Now Allah ﷻ tells the worshippers: they’re calling you people liars. Everything you’re saying, they’re saying it’s all a lie.

فَمَا تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفًا وَلَا نَصْرًا – then you’re not going to be able now to ward it off, to get away, nor can you find any legal counsel, any aid, any protection.

The scene then pulls back from Judgment Day to address us now: وَمَن يَظْلِم مِّنكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذَابًا كَبِيرًا – whoever does wrong among you, I will make him taste a huge punishment.

This is a teaching technique. The ayah played this entire scene from the Day of Judgment – from the opening of this section about the Fire seeing them, to them being thrown in, to the interrogation of those they worshipped. Then it pauses and brings us back to the present: “By the way, that scene you just witnessed? Whoever is wrong among you, there won’t be an exception. I will make him taste that huge punishment.”

It’s like a teacher showing a video about the deteriorated lungs of someone who smoked extensively, then pausing and saying: “You guys going to smoke? Because if you’re going to smoke, this exact thing is going to happen. Science doesn’t change.” This is like that. The ayah played the scene, then pulled back: this is real, this is your future if you choose wrong.

Ayah 20

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ ۗ وَجَعَلْنَا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً أَتَصْبِرُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيرًا

Remember their criticism: “This messenger eats food and walks in the markets”? Allah ﷻ responds: وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ – We never sent any messengers before you إِلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ – except that they used to eat food وَيَمْشُونَ فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ – and they used to walk around in the markets too.

This is what messengers do. They’re human. They have human needs. That doesn’t diminish their message or their status.

وَجَعَلْنَا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً – and We have made some of you a trial for others. This is crucial. Some people start getting impressed with the message of Islam. Then they go hang out with their friends, and their friends say: “You’re seriously thinking about Islam? That guy is the messenger? I just saw him eating the other day. He’s a human being just like us. Why are you thinking he’s got something special going on? Have you seen the angel? Get real. If Allah ﷻ sent an angel, we’d know. Angels don’t need food. They don’t need to go shopping. If we saw an angel come, that would be different. Until you see the angel, don’t worry about this stuff. Don’t get involved in this Islam thing.”

So some people become trials for others. Allah ﷻ asks: أَتَصْبِرُونَ – will you be patient? Will you stay strong with your religion? Don’t be tempted. Don’t give in.

Your friends will say “Stay patient with your religion. Hold on to it. Don’t be tempted by this cult of Islam.” And Allah ﷻ responds: what about you and your so-called patience?

وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيرًا – your Master has been in full view of this entire scene. He sees everything. He knows who’s genuine and who’s playing games with faith.

Ayah 21

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا ۗ لَقَدِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْ عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا

وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا – those who are not hopeful of meeting Us, people who don’t believe in an afterlife and don’t care about it, can see all kinds of things to invalidate religious arguments.

They come up and say: لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ – how come angels weren’t sent down on us? أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا – or better yet, why can’t we just see our Master? Allah ﷻ is so interested in talking to me, why can’t He just show up and talk to me? You know, make Himself available.

لَقَدِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ – they are seeking to declare their own greatness. They are so full of themselves. Deep inside is a very high form of arrogance. Allah ﷻ has to come to you? You are too high to go to Allah ﷻ? That’s the problem here.

Angels should come to you? They should serve you? They should meet your demands? As though you’re in a position to make demands. That’s the attitude problem.

وَعَتَوْ عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا – they have engaged in treachery that comes from arrogance. They’ve engaged in a crime as a result of their pride. When you think too highly of yourself, you end up making huge mistakes. They’ve made a colossal, arrogant mistake.

Ayah 22

يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا

You want to see angels? يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ – the Day on which they will see the angels لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ – won’t be a day of good news for the criminals.

When the angels do show up and they start coming, you turn around to them and say “You wanted to see the angels, right? Well, here they are. You wanted to see them? Where’s your attitude now?”

They used to mock: “Yeah, it would be so awesome to see an angel.” Allah ﷻ says there won’t be celebration that day. They’re not going to be congratulating each other and high-fiving.

وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا – and they will say “Please, no, no, no!”

حِجْر has several interpretations. It means rock, also a barrier. It’s used like this: when an army would be advancing and you knew that army was way stronger than you, you had no way to be saved, you would make a desperate prayer: حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا – may Allah ﷻ descend a giant boulder between us and them, and keep crushing them with these boulders so they don’t reach me.

It’s also used to mean “prevent them from moving forward” – like when someone’s about to recite something evil to you, you say حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا: “Stop yourself. Don’t hurt me. Go away. Get away.”

On that Day, they’ll be saying the same thing. All the arrogance is gone. The same people who said “Can we just see Allah ﷻ? Can Allah ﷻ just come and talk to me?” – that same person is now begging: “Please, stop! Please don’t! Stop, please, please, please stop!”

Ayah 23

وَقَدِمْنَآ إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هَبَآءً مَّنثُورًا

This is the third time this concept appears in the Quran. I’ll translate first, then explain the context.

وَقَدِمْنَآ إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ مِنْ عَمَلٍ – We will come to whatever they had done. قَدِمْنَا literally means to come to something to observe it, to evaluate it, to appraise it.

Think about relatable examples. You’ve seen a science fair – judges come and walk over to your display, look at it, examine it, evaluate it. Or someone trying to sell gold – the appraiser comes and looks at it to determine its worth. A real estate buyer comes to your house to check it out. Someone’s buying your car and comes to inspect it.

When someone has made something – manufactured something, built something – and you go examine their work, you’re examining not just a thing but their work itself. Like when people make pottery. It’s literally their work sitting on the table. A customer walks over, picks it up, examines it: “Okay, so there’s a little bulge here, it’s uneven there,” looks at it carefully, then gives it a value: “I’ll give you five for it. I’ll give you two for it.”

Allah ﷻ Himself will come as the Evaluator of whatever they had done. قَدِمْنَآ – We will literally take steps toward what they have done. Every single deed they had done.

فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هَبَآءً مَّنثُورًا – We will turn every one of those deeds into scattered dust. هَبَآء means tiny particles of dust, like those fiber things you see floating in sunlight when you look out a window on a sunny day. If you’re weird, you try to catch them.

مَنثُور means spread in every direction. You shake out sheets and dust flies everywhere – that’s مَنثُور. Or blowing your nose and stuff comes out in all directions – that’s also مَنثُور.

Allah ﷻ is saying: the disbeliever did some good deeds too. This person who said “How come Allah ﷻ doesn’t come talk to me?” – he also helped out at a blood drive. He donated to charity. He ran for cancer awareness. He’s got a pile of those good deeds.

The Master comes to appraise them. Every single deed – any good deed at any point in their life, because everybody does something good – Allah ﷻ will pick every one of those things out and say: “This is worth… scattered dust. That’s all it’s worth.”

This is the third time this idea is mentioned. The first two times, it was mentioned as a parable, as a comparison. The third time – here – it’s literal.

The first time was in Surah Ibrahim: their deeds on Judgment Day are like ash that the wind blows away. Ash itself is worthless, and when wind blows it away, you can’t even recover it.

The second time was in the previous surah, Surah An-Nur: the thirsty person thinks a mirage is water, gets there and finds nothing. That was still a parable.

The third time, here, it’s literal. If those two times didn’t work for you, let me spell it out like it is. All the things you think you’ve done that are good – because people think “I’m good, I don’t need faith to be good, I’m good on my own merits” – Allah ﷻ says: let me tell you how much that goodness is worth: هَبَآءً مَّنثُورًا – scattered dust.

The farther we get from the Quran, the more we’ll start having doubts, the more we’ll start thinking “What about all those other people?” These ayahs don’t mean you go around telling people their lives aren’t worth anything. The best of you in Islam – those same people, when they become Muslim, Allah ﷻ removes all their bad deeds but keeps all the good they’ve done. He multiplies it on top of that.

We’re not here to judge where people are going on Judgment Day. Allah ﷻ is there to do that. We don’t do the judging. We share the word of Allah ﷻ so people can do the judging for themselves and can know that Allah ﷻ has passed a verdict.

There’s a difference between the Quran speaking and us speaking to someone. The Prophet ﷺ was the nicest person – you’ve read this so many times. He’s the nicest person. Can you imagine the nicest person going around saying harsh things to people? But it’s not him talking, it’s Allah ﷻ telling him to say this harsh thing.

We cannot assume the position of the Master when we speak. We should speak as slaves. When we speak on behalf of the Quran, the Quran is the speech of the Master. We can’t be condescending to others. He can be – He’s the Master. He can be authoritative. He can be harsh if He wants. He can scold. He has the right. We’re His slaves.

It’s His book, His word, to these subjects on this earth who are rebelling against Him. If He speaks harshly in the Quran and somebody says “Your Quran is so harsh,” the answer is: yes, it’s Allah ﷻ, He has a right. You don’t complain about the cop who pulls you over: “You’re so mean, you pulled me over!” You don’t do that because you know he has authority.

Allah ﷻ is way more merciful than any cop. He didn’t stop you. He just said don’t do it. And if you stop now, I’ll cancel all your tickets. On top of that, I’ll reward you. And you complain?

Ayah 24

أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا

أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ – the companions of Jannah, the people who hang out in Jannah on that Day. صُحْبَة means company. They’re always in good company, always hanging out there.

خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا – they’re going to be better off as far as مُسْتَقَر. مُسْتَقَر is a place to chill, a place to cool off, a place to relax.

وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا – and it’s better as a time to just sit back and enjoy your day. مَقِيل specifically refers to an afternoon rest. In Arabic, it comes from قَيْلُولَة – to take an afternoon relaxation or rest.

Actually, مَقِيل is significant because there’s no sleep in Jannah. You don’t need sleep. But you can chill any time you want, sit back whenever you want. Sleep means you lost time, time you could have done something else. But in Jannah you don’t lose energy, you don’t get tired, you don’t need sleep. But you want to sit around? You sure can.

There’s a joke I love about this. A young man is sitting on a bench in a park in the middle of the day. An old man walks by and says: “Young man, why are you sitting there? You should do something!”

“What should I do?”

“Sell fruit! Get a cart, get some fruit and sell it.”

“Then what?”

“If you start selling bananas, you can add apples.”

“What after that?”

“You can add oranges.”

“What after that?”

“If it goes well, you can open a second cart.”

“What after that?”

“A third cart, fourth cart, fifth cart. You could have an entire franchise. You could be a millionaire selling fruits.”

“What do I do after all that?”

“Then you can relax.”

“Thanks. I’m already relaxing.”

This also tells us: in Jannah you will relax. This is not the time to relax. This is the time to work. This is the time to get busy. This is the time to earn the Jannah.

Ayah 25

وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِٱلْغَمَـٰمِ وَنُزِّلَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا

وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِٱلْغَمَـٰمِ – the Day on which We tear the sky open, crack it open with these dark clouds.

وَنُزِّلَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا – and the angels start getting sent down. Notice the form: نُزِّلَ… تَنزِيلًا. Allah ﷻ doesn’t just say the angels will be sent down. The form suggests row upon row upon row, little by little, continuously. It’ll feel like they never stop. They just keep coming.

When an army is shown to be intimidating, you see the first row appear, then the second row, then the third row, and you think “Three rows, that’s pretty impressive.” Then the fourth shows up, the fifth, the sixth. It just doesn’t end. You get overwhelmed. This is نُزِّلَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا.

Notice the sentence doesn’t even finish. “The Day on which this happens…” and then what? When the “then” portion is left unsaid, it’s because what comes next is unspeakable. Words won’t do justice to what’s going to come. Use your imagination, because description fails.

Ayah 26

ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَسِيرًا

ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ – the dominion on that Day, the true dominion, is truly going to belong to Ar-Rahman. The true kingdom on that day belongs to the Most Merciful.

وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَسِيرًا – and that has always been meant to be a Day that is going to be very difficult on the disbelievers.

Ayah 27

وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ ٱلظَّالِمُ عَلَىٰ يَدَيْهِ يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى ٱتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ ٱلرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا

وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ ٱلظَّالِمُ عَلَىٰ يَدَيْهِ – the Day on which the wrongdoer, the evildoer, the criminal, the sinner is going to be biting on his hands.

In old times, this was done as an expression. When people are really nervous, they have nervous gestures. On that Day, this is the expression. He’s going to be biting on his hands – not just biting nails, literally biting the fingers off. That’s how nervous, how shocked he’s going to be.

يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى ٱتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ ٱلرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا – and he’s going to say: “Oh! If only I had taken a path alongside the Messenger! If only I had taken the road the Messenger was taking!”

Remember they were saying “How come no angel walks around with him?” Now they say: I should have been the one walking around with him. I should have been taking a road with him.

The word سَبِيل here creates beautiful imagery. The Prophet ﷺ himself is on a journey, on a mission in this world that needed to be completed. The regret is: I should have been part of that mission. I should have been there with him.

Ayah 28

يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰ لَيْتَنِى لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا

يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰ – oh my! Oh God! لَيْتَنِى لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا – if only I didn’t take that guy – just “that guy,” فُلَان – as my خَلِيل.

He can’t even remember his name. What’s his name? I wish I didn’t have him as a friend.

خَلِيل is a close friend, a loving friend, someone you turn to in times of trouble. When you’re in trouble and you think of someone, that’s your خَلِيل. Someone you text: “Can we talk right now?” If you do that, and they’re there for you, that’s your خَلِيل.

We’re learning about opposites. You had a friend who was your خَلِيل – you turned to him on the most personal matters. You took his counsel on religion too. You didn’t take the message seriously, didn’t take Allah ﷻ seriously, didn’t take the Messenger ﷺ seriously because of that guy. Because your friend influenced you.

When you get to Judgment Day, that really close friend – these are opposites – if he’s your خَلِيل, you should at least know his name, know who he is. On Judgment Day, he becomes فُلَان – “that guy,” “so-and-so,” “John Doe.” Even the person talking can’t remember: what was that guy’s name? Why did I have him as a friend? I don’t even remember who he was.

Ayah 29

لَّقَدْ أَضَلَّنِى عَنِ ٱلذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَنِى ۗ وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ خَذُولًا

لَّقَدْ أَضَلَّنِى عَنِ ٱلذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَنِى – he’s the one who misled me, took me away from the remembrance after it had come to me. I had the opportunity to remember Allah ﷻ. I had the opportunity to learn His book.

And he said: “No, bro, come on, Batman just came out. Let’s go see that. Then we’ll watch Spider-Man. Then whatever else we can sneak into. By that time, ’Isha will be done and we can head back to the masjid.”

لَّقَدْ أَضَلَّنِى عَنِ ٱلذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَنِى.

وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ خَذُولًا – and Shaytan, especially for the human being, has always been extremely deceptive as a friend.

خَذُول means the friend who shows you he’s a friend, he’s with you all the time. But when trouble shows up and you really need him, he disappears. He shows a complete lack of loyalty at the crucial moment. Just when you need him most, he says: “I’m not with you. What are you talking about? I’m not your friend.”

“You backstabbing… why did you…”

“Don’t yell at me. Let’s just burn together. Worry about it now. There’s no point yelling at me now.”

A lot of people will turn on him. But Shaytan says: “Look, listen, I’m خَذُول. I act like your friend, but can you really rely on me at the end of the day? That’s your problem. You didn’t know I’m like that? I didn’t tell you otherwise.”

Shaytan’s going to trash talk on Judgment Day too. He’s going to say: “Please don’t yell at me. I’m trying to burn in peace over here. Let’s just burn, okay?”

Ayah 30

وَقَالَ ٱلرَّسُولُ يَـٰرَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِى ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ مَهْجُورًا

And if your problems weren’t bad enough already: وَقَالَ ٱلرَّسُولُ – the Messenger ﷺ will say يَـٰرَبِّ – Ya Rabbi, my Master إِنَّ قَوْمِى – this nation of mine, my nation ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ مَهْجُورًا – no doubt they took this Quran and abandoned it. They left it in abandonment.

The previous person said “I didn’t take a path with the Messenger.” And the

Ayah 33–60 · linked video: 02. Al-Furqan (Ayah 33-60)

Surah Al-Furqan: Ayahs 33-60

Ayah 33

وَلَا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ إِلَّا جِئْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيرًا

This ayah responds to the complaint raised by the disbelievers in the previous passage about why the Quran wasn’t revealed all at once. Allah ﷻ addresses their criticism directly: “They don’t bring you any kind of theory, hypothesis, or case study—whether it’s ‘Why is the Quran this way?’ or ‘Why isn’t it that way?’—except that We deliver the truth to you.”

The disbelievers constantly presented arguments: “How come the Quran came down in pieces? Why wasn’t it revealed to one of the two celebrities in town?” They reasoned that if the revelation had come to someone already famous in Makkah, people would have listened more readily. But Allah ﷻ responds to every one of these theories with truth—the correct response to their nonsense.

The phrase وَأَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيرًا means Allah ﷻ gives the best possible explanation and rationale for why things are the way they are. Every time revelation comes down, it addresses a real problem at the right occasion. When the Sahaba (RA) asked a question, when missionaries from Najran came asking about their faith in Isa (AS), at that very moment Allah ﷻ would reveal the ayahs necessary to engage in that conversation.

This gradual revelation served a profound purpose: it prompted the Messenger ﷺ for the right conversation at the right time, constantly reaffirming his heart that Allah ﷻ was by his side. Every new revelation reignited his faith and enthusiasm for the work of dawah.

The word فُؤَاد refers to an overwhelmed or excited heart. The Messenger ﷺ had incredible enthusiasm and energy for delivering this message, but anyone doing work for Islam—whether youth work, lectures, volunteering at humanitarian organizations—faces the possibility of burning out. The Prophet ﷺ didn’t have weekends off; his was more than full-time work. He needed something to keep his energy and enthusiasm going, and so every few days or on significant occasions, Allah ﷻ revealed more Quran to rejuvenate his spirit and make him firm again.

The ayah concludes with وَرَتَّلْنَاهُ تَرْتِيلًا—“We made sure that it gets recited in a slow, rhythmic, melodious, calculated fashion.” Tarteel means to enunciate every letter, to execute a word smoothly, to have measure and melody in how you speak. Not only would the Quran not come down all at once, but even a single sentence shouldn’t be blurted out. It should be done with tarteel, taking time with each syllable.

Ayah 34

ٱلَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا

Those who mock the revelation and refuse to accept it will be herded on their faces all the way to Jahannam—dragged on their faces toward Hell. These are the people far worse off in terms of their final place.

The phrase وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا—“way more lost in terms of a path”—implies they keep trying to find a way out of Hell but keep getting lost. They’re trapped in a labyrinth with no escape. On the Day of Judgment, they’ll try to walk toward where the believers are walking, but they won’t be given that path. They keep getting lost and end up exactly where they’re supposed to end up.

This serves as a stark contrast: the disbelievers complain about the path of revelation, calling the Messenger ﷺ misguided, when in reality they are the ones hopelessly lost.

Ayah 35

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلْنَا مَعَهُۥٓ أَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ وَزِيرًا

We had truly already given Musa (AS) the Book, and We made with him his brother Harun (AS) as a wazir—someone who carries his burden, someone who backs him up.

This begins a unique feature of this surah. Usually when a prophet’s story is mentioned, at least two or three ayahs detail what happened: this occurred, then that happened, and finally that nation was destroyed. But in this surah, the pattern is abrupt: We sent a messenger, and We destroyed that nation. Beginning and ending, nothing in between.

That abruptness exists because the Makkan disbelievers are being told: it happened quickly. You have very little time before that happens to you.

Ayah 36

فَقُلْنَا ٱذْهَبَآ إِلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهُمْ تَدْمِيرًا

Then We said, “Both of you go to the nation that has called Our miraculous signs a lie.” Then We crushed them completely.

The word تَدْمِيرًا is powerful—it implies waves crashing down on them, piled one on top of the other, complete and utter destruction.

Ayah 37

وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ لَّمَّا كَذَّبُوا۟ ٱلرُّسُلَ أَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ ءَايَةً ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا

And the nation of Nuh (AS)—when they lied against the messengers, We drowned them. Look at the example: Nuh (AS) spent 950 years calling his people, yet the Quran gets immediately to the conclusion of the story. We drowned them.

وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ ءَايَةً - We made them a miraculous sign, an ayah for people. Some scholars argue that Mount Judi has been found and remnants of the Ark discovered. Rather than relying on unreliable blog sources, this is why Muslim youth need to give rise to an entire genre of Quranic studies—exploring Quranic history, sociology, political science, psychology through university-level research. I’m very confident this will happen in America, insha’Allah.

وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا - Allah ﷻ prepared for the wrongdoers a painful punishment.

Ayah 38

وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَا۟ وَأَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلرَّسِّ وَقُرُونًۢا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ كَثِيرًا

And the people of Ad, Thamud, and the people of Ar-Rass—we don’t know much about these people. Some speculate they took their messenger, locked him up, threw him in a well, and killed him that way, or imprisoned him there. The word رَسّ in Arabic refers to a well.

وَقُرُونًۢا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ كَثِيرًا—and generations upon generations of people in between as well, so many of them.

Ayah 39

وَكُلًّا ضَرَبْنَا لَهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ ۖ وَكُلًّا تَبَّرْنَا تَتْبِيرًا

All of these nations—messengers came and delivered messages. All of that We have done; We gave all of those as examples. Every single one of those nations, We crushed and annihilated completely.

These stories of the prophets serve to firm the Prophet’s ﷺ heart. The point is to let the Messenger ﷺ know: you’re not the first to be in this position, and you’re not the last. So many before you made the same exact struggles you’re making, and they were always given victory in the end. You will be too.

That’s why these stories are given over and over again. The one commonality within all of them: maybe their criticisms were different, maybe the messengers and miracles given were different, but the destruction at the end was always constant.

Ayah 40

وَلَقَدْ أَتَوْا۟ عَلَى ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُمْطِرَتْ مَطَرَ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۚ أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ يَرَوْنَهَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَرْجُونَ نُشُورًا

They did pass by the nation that had been showered upon with an evil kind of shower—perhaps referring to the nation of Lut (AS). Didn’t they used to look at that? Didn’t they used to just look at it and wonder what happened here?

But even if they did look, they’re not the kind of people who would think about or hope that they’re going to be raised from the dead again. That’s why when they look at archaeological sites—these tourist locations where excavations happen—they don’t learn anything about the Hereafter from them.

When nations hundreds or thousands of years old are discovered, when volcanic eruptions turned entire bodies into statues of ash and those things are dug up, they’re supposed to be an ibrah—a lesson. What nation was this? What must they have done for Allah ﷻ to annihilate them this way? It’s not just interesting history. It’s supposed to be a real warning.

Ayah 41

وَإِذَا رَأَوْكَ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى بَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولًا

Whenever they look at you, they see you, they take you for nothing but a joke. “This guy? He’s the one that Allah appointed as a messenger? Really?”

Ayah 42

إِن كَادَ لَيُضِلُّنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا لَوْلَآ أَن صَبَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ حِينَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا

“It was almost the case—he almost got us too. He almost misled us from our gods. He almost got us to leave our religious tradition, had it not been that we were committed and patient and held strong to our values. He almost got us. He was a threat to our sovereignty, a threat to our religious identity. Thank God we held on the way we held on.”

Then soon they’re going to see when they’re looking at the punishment—who’s more misguided in terms of a path?

This is deeply sad because they continuously called the Prophet ﷺ misguided. Notice this rhetoric survived through the ages against Islam. No other religion will there be rhetoric against in this way. People don’t campaign throughout history against Hinduism or Buddhism, calling them false, evil, saying “we have to stop it, we have to end it.”

But with Islam, you’ll find it throughout the ages—different people of different religions coming at it as though it’s this evil thing, as though these people are misguided and they’re there to mess up your society.

If such thoughts start crossing your mind—“Well, if so many people think we’re messed up…”—it’s like thinking, “If everybody says I’m crazy, maybe I’m not quite totally there. If everybody hates Islam, there’s got to be something wrong with us. How can all these people be wrong?”

The answer to that is in the Quran: إِن تُطِعْ أَكْثَرَ مَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يُضِلُّوكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ (6:116)—“If you were to follow the majority on the earth, they will mislead you from the path of Allah ﷻ.”

The path of Allah ﷻ is meant for a minority, because only the minority is brave enough to withstand the criticism of the rest of the world. The fact that they’re criticizing Islam, Allah ﷻ says, is validation that you are the right one, not the wrong one. Fighting the pressures of a majority can be very difficult at a personal level and even at the level of a nation.

Ayah 43

أَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ أَفَأَنتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلًا

Have you ever looked at someone who took his whims and desires and turned them into his god?

What does it mean to take your desires and turn them into your god? An ilah is someone you obey, someone whose authority you accept as absolute—not just someone you worship, but someone you give into. Have you seen someone who submits to their whims as though they should be submitting to Allah ﷻ? Whenever an urge comes to them, they impulsively follow that urge. They’ve completely given themselves up before that urge.

This is اتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ. Are you going to take care of a person like that? You’re going to worry about his matter? He’s a lost cause.

A person like that doesn’t have to have an idol in front of him—the idol sits inside his heart. His greed, his money is his god. His addiction is his god. His temptation is his god. His reputation is his god. Whatever he’s obsessed with or she’s obsessed with, they start worshiping it—meaning they give in to that. That’s all they do. That’s all they live for.

Ilah is what becomes the goal of your life. Our goal is Allah ﷻ. What’s the goal of your life?

Ayah 44

أَمْ تَحْسَبُ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ أَوْ يَعْقِلُونَ ۚ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا

Do you really think that most of them are actually listening? Or that they even understand? They’re not.

These people are nothing but the example of cattle—they’re just like cattle. It’s like you’re giving a lecture to cows and sheep. When people are completely self-absorbed (ayah 43 describes people who are utterly self-absorbed), they become a hopeless cause. Even if they listen to a reminder about the real Ilah, it doesn’t go anywhere because there’s no space for another ilah inside. They don’t have any room left, so they can’t hear you. Nothing processes.

إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا—they’re nothing but the likes of cattle, or even worse off than cattle. At least cattle might listen and turn your way, might move back in response. These people? Nothing. Silence.

Ayah 45

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ ٱلظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَجَعَلَهُۥ سَاكِنًا ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلًا

Didn’t you look toward your Master? How He extends the shadow?

The word ظِلّ comes from ظُلمة—darkness. Dark clouds are also called dhull. The imagery here is how Allah ﷻ extends the shadows. Shadows remind you that darkness used to be here. They’re a reminder of the night. When shadows extend, you know night is about to fall.

وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَجَعَلَهُۥ سَاكِنًا—Had Allah ﷻ wanted, He would have made the shade permanent. In other words, He would have made darkness permanent.

This is giving the example of revelation coming. Didn’t you see how the light has come all around? But some people still want to remain inside the darkness, don’t want to come out into the light. Had Allah ﷻ wanted, He would have never sent guidance at all. They should appreciate it so much.

ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلًا—then We made the sun a proof, a means to appreciate what it means to be in darkness. The sun came and showed you, by opposites. Things are known through their opposites. The sun became proof of darkness. If you only knew darkness, you wouldn’t even know there’s such a thing as light. The sun came and showed: Oh, darkness is the absence of something way better—light itself.

This compares light and day to Islam, and night to kufr. Islam is like day; night is like disbelief. If somebody only knew night, they wouldn’t even know there’s such a thing as light. Imagine that—even though day came, they never opened the curtains. They stay in the shade. These people don’t want to come out and experience the revelation of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 46

ثُمَّ قَبَضْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْنَا قَبْضًا يَسِيرًا

Then We grab the shadow and We start pulling it back toward Ourselves very easily.

The rise and fall between truth and falsehood is a continual struggle.

Ayah 47

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا وَٱلنَّوْمَ سُبَاتًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ نُشُورًا

He’s the one who made the night into a garment for you, an outfit for you. Like it covers you—clothes you put on yourself, you cover yourself with them. The night covers you. It’s like this gigantic blanket that takes over the planet.

وَٱلنَّوْمَ سُبَاتًا—and the sleep as a means of rest and recovery for you. One thing about سُبَات: it doesn’t just mean rest. It means to be at real peace. Sometimes you rest and wake up disturbed. Sometimes you rest and wake up deeply calm, super peaceful. That kind of peace is subat.

وَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ نُشُورًا—and He made the day a means by which you can revive yourself, come back to life. نُشُور is used—coming back from the dead. Here it’s coming back from sleep, but it also implies resurrection.

Ayah 48

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً طَهُورًا

He’s the one who sent the winds as a means of good news right before His mercy—right in front of His mercy, or right ahead of His mercy.

The winds are coming, and you think, “Oh, I can tell it’s going to rain. These kinds of winds—I can tell clouds are going to be right behind. Something good is going to happen.”

Allah ﷻ is saying: in everything else you can sense—if you have a good awareness of the world around you—you can tell by the signs that something better is ahead.

This imagery also alludes to the signs of the coming of the Prophet ﷺ. There were certain signs that people of knowledge, people of the Book, saw happening even before the Prophet ﷺ was born. They said, “Winds of revelation coming from the sky are here.” Not rain from the sky this time, but revelation from the sky. “We can tell certain major events are happening.”

That’s why people like Bahira were anticipating the coming of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. They were waiting for it. The Jews were waiting for it. They used to go into battle with the mushrikeen and lose most of the time, and as they were walking away, they’d say, “All the signs of the last messenger are here, and he’s been guaranteed victory. You guys just watch when he comes.” كَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ (2:89) - They used to seek victory through him against those who disbelieved.

The signs were there before the Prophet ﷺ came. The winds were there before the rain, before His mercy. The winds show up, and then Allah ﷻ sends purifying water from the sky, just like revelation came purifying.

Ayah 49

لِّنُحْـِۧىَ بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا وَنُسْقِيَهُۥ مِمَّا خَلَقْنَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا وَأَنَاسِىَّ كَثِيرًا

So We can give life by means of it to a land that is death itself. Not مَيِّت (dead), but مَيْتًا—death itself. This land was so dead, it had no guidance for centuries. It had nothing in it, no resources. We brought it back to life.

وَنُسْقِيَهُۥ مِمَّا خَلَقْنَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا وَأَنَاسِىَّ كَثِيرًا—and We can give drink from it even to what We created: cattle and so many people.

Just like water feeds all creation, the revelation came and started giving sustenance to all creation and so many nations of people. أَنَاسِىّ is argued to be the plural of نَاس. So nas is the plural of إنسان, and nas itself has a plural. This indicates multitudes of people, different nations of people, different tribes of people. Some scholars argue it means different kinds of people, different kinds of personalities.

Ayah 50

وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَـٰهُ بَيْنَهُمْ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟ فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا

We have changed it among them—صَرَّفْنَـٰهُ بَيْنَهُمْ means We’ve changed the winds this way and that way. It also means We’ve changed the Quran. We’ve changed the lesson: We teach it this way, then We change it, then We teach it that way. We alternate. Maybe this just didn’t click with you—maybe this will click with you.

لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟—so they may make some effort to remember.

فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا—but most people are bent upon disbelieving. أَبَىٰ indicates when you’re bent upon doing something, you won’t accept any alternative. Most people are bent upon being disbelievers and being ungrateful.

Some scholars also say the هُ (it) refers to the water. When Allah ﷻ sends water down, sometimes He changes the location of where it should come down. When the land doesn’t get water for a long time, it gets thirsty, and people start begging for water. In different ancient civilizations, when water was awaited, they had some kind of prayer or spiritual dance to get the water to come. Even they realized we have to call on a higher power for this to come.

Keeping with the parable: revelation hasn’t come for a long time, and humanity should be desperate like they’re in a drought. They should beg Allah ﷻ for that revelation to come. It finally came, but most people are bent upon being ungrateful.

Ayah 51

وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَبَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ نَّذِيرًا

Had We wanted, We would have appointed a warner—a messenger—in every single village on the planet. Every single village, every single zip code would have had a messenger.

But Allah ﷻ says no, that’s not the case. We learn later that messengers are sent to capitals, to central cities, to cultural headquarters, to civilizational headquarters. Why? Because everybody else copies them.

When I tell my friends in New York I live in Euless, Texas, they say, “Why do you say useless Texas? Do they have gas stations there?” They don’t even know what New Jersey is—if you live in South Jersey, you’re in the boonies. You know nothing about Mississippi or Louisiana.

Even here, far from New York, I drive by and see “New York Pizza,” “Chicago Pizza.” Food culture is borrowed from centers, from capital cultures. Messengers were sent to places where the action is, because just like the heart feeds the rest of the body blood, the heart of a nation is the central city. The central cities feed the rest of that nation its economy, its culture, its education, its values. They come from those cultural capitals.

You have to be where the action is. Messengers were not sent to any unnamed village. They were sent to the important places of their time.

Ayah 52

فَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَجَـٰهِدْهُم بِهِۦ جِهَادًا كَبِيرًا

Then don’t be influenced by the disbelievers. It doesn’t just mean don’t disobey them—don’t be influenced by these disbelievers.

وَجَـٰهِدْهُم بِهِۦ—and struggle against them with it. Jihad against them with it—“it” being the Quran.

جِهَادًا كَبِيرًا—the great jihad.

If somebody asks, “You Muslims, you believe in jihad, right?” You say, “No, we don’t just believe in jihad. We believe in the great jihad.” “The great jihad? What’s that?” “Yeah, it’s in the Quran. I’ll show you.” Right here, in Surah Al-Furqan.

Between the fifth and eighth years of the Makkan period—was any fighting happening at that time? No. But the command from Allah ﷻ to His Messenger ﷺ: make jihad with the Quran, a great jihad.

What’s the great jihad? Jihad actually means to struggle against an opposing force with everything you have. You’re struggling against someone with everything you’ve got. Now everything you’ve got, according to this ayah, is what? The Quran. Struggle against the opposition with the Quran.

There are going to be opposing forces. The struggle to deliver the message to as many people as possible, to respond to their criticisms, to engage in conversation with them as Allah ﷻ wants us to engage—that’s one dimension.

Then there’s tarbiyah: people have accepted the message, but that’s not enough. They have to grow, mature, cultivate. The Quran will be that which you struggle with to bring them up to speed, to grow them, to sprout them. The Quran will be a means not just of dawah, but also of tarbiyah.

Then it’s a means of tazkiyah: not only will these people mature and develop in their understanding, they’re going to be cleansed by means of it, remain pure in their intentions. Because when you work on something for a long time, your intentions can get corrupt. The Quran will keep your intentions clean.

These are just some dimensions of jihad bil Quran. You’re struggling with the Quran—with the society around you and with yourselves—with a heavy jihad, a great jihad.

Don’t be influenced. What they say—the Quran will keep you on track.

Ayah 53

۞ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَرَجَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ هَـٰذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ وَهَـٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا

He’s the one who caused two oceans to merge into one another, to smash into one another. This one is عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ—so sweet, cool water, the kind of water that even if you’re not thirsty, you want to drink it and you can’t stop drinking it. فُرَات means huge in quantity, overflowing.

وَهَـٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ—and this one is bad-tasting, gives you heartburn. It’s salty.

Salty water and fresh water are slamming into each other. Do they mix? No.

وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا—We placed between them a barrier that can’t be crossed.

وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا—and it’s as though a boulder has been dropped from the sky that is preventing one from reaching the other. They’re completely safe from one another, completely prevented from one another.

Two kinds of water—salty and sweet—have come together. This ayah comes in a passage where Allah ﷻ says, “Don’t be influenced by the disbelievers.” The mushrikeen don’t just come and say, “Leave Islam.” They say, “We’re worried about you. We’re worried about Quraysh. If outsiders find out we’re having internal struggles, they might make a power play against Makkah itself. What you’re doing is threatening the entire family, the entire tribe. Muhammad, we’re not against you. We don’t hate you. We’re looking out for you. Let’s just compromise. Let’s work something out.”

Allah ﷻ says: sweet water and salty water will slam into each other, but there is a wall between them, a barrier. Don’t be influenced by them. This is how you are: you are the fresh water, they are the salty water. You may be together, but there’s not going to be any compromise. There’s not going to be a little bit of fresh and salty at the same time. It’s not going to happen.

Just because you’re in the same tribe, they’ll come and say, “We’re the same tribe. We raised you. You’re from our people. How can you do this? How could you do this to us?” Yeah, we’re both water, that’s for sure. But there is a wall between us, and that wall is Islam itself. It separated us.

The surah started by saying تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ—how blessed is the One who sent down the thing that divides. Look at this parallel: it’s dividing two things, dividing truth and falsehood, like two kinds of water.

Some people discuss this as a scientific miracle of the Quran, saying the Prophet ﷺ never saw fresh water and seawater merging together, rivers merging into oceans. He never experienced this, yet he knew about it. People who know marine life or live by the sea or rivers have seen this with their own eyes, so this is a miracle of the Quran.

That’s true, but if that’s all you talk about, you missed the point. This is part of the speech. Allah ﷻ didn’t just mention this as an interesting fact. He brought this up in the conversation of struggle between truth and falsehood—that truth shouldn’t be influenced by falsehood. That’s why studying the Quran in flow is so important. Going through a surah as a unit of conversation is essential, because you miss out on what Allah ﷻ is saying from beginning to end. It’s one speech.

Ayah 54

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ بَشَرًا فَجَعَلَهُۥ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا

He’s the one who created from a kind of water the human being. The makeup of the human being—we are 90% water.

The other meaning: the fluid, the nutfah I discussed previously in Surah Nun—we’re created from a fluid.

فَجَعَلَهُۥ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا—He’s the one who made family relations by means of that fluid. This refers to blood relations through the mother’s womb, and also relations through in-laws.

Interestingly, this comes right before and right after the mention of sweet water and salt water—the twain shall never meet. The surah began with الْفُرْقَانَ—the criterion that separates. Yet Allah ﷻ made relations that are blood relations, and He made relations by you marrying into another family—in-laws.

The word صِهْر also means to burn in Arabic. I don’t know the connection and won’t use my imagination, but it’s interesting.

وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا—and your Master has always been completely capable.

Ayah 55

وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُهُمْ وَلَا يَضُرُّهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱلْكَافِرُ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ ظَهِيرًا

They worship, they enslave themselves to other than Allah ﷻ—things that don’t benefit them and don’t harm them.

وَكَانَ ٱلْكَافِرُ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ ظَهِيرًا—and the disbeliever, when it came to his Master, has turned his back, has permanently turned his back.

If the ayah used لِ with ظَهِير, it would mean backing someone up. ظَهِير comes from ظَهْر, which means back. When ظَهِير is used with لِ, it means you’re backing someone up, you’ve got their back: “I got your back, bro.” That’s ظَهِير لَكَ—“I got your back, I’m right behind you, I’m covering you.”

But this ayah says كَانَ ٱلْكَافِرُ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ ظَهِيرًا—not لِ but عَلَىٰ. This means the disbeliever has turned his back against Allah ﷻ, and he’s permanently turned his back. ظَهِير is an adjective that means either “I always have your back” or “I’ve permanently turned my back.”

The disbeliever has permanently turned his back.

Ayah 56

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا

The previous ayah says the disbelievers have permanently turned their backs. So what’s the point of giving a message if they’re never going to turn around?

Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ: We have not sent you for any reason except to give good news and to warn.

You don’t worry about what people do. That’s My problem. You just keep giving good news and you keep warning. If you don’t see changes in people, don’t worry about it. That’s not your job. Your job is to give good news and give warning.

Ayah 57

قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ أَن يَتَّخِذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا

Tell them: I’m not asking you for any compensation over the Quran, this message.

The word مَا is used here—a negation that implies they said something and he’s refuting them. What they said doesn’t even have to be spelled out because the word ma itself carries that implication. They accused: “He wants us to believe in him as a messenger because he wants money. He wants control. He wants power. He knows how important this city is. If he’s the mayor of this city, that’s big. He’s got some alternative agenda he’s not telling us about.”

So the Messenger ﷺ is told to declare openly: مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ—I’m not asking for any compensation, any form of compensation.

There’s another layer here. They called him a poet, a magician, a soothsayer. In Arab culture, when a magician finishes his show, he says, “Any contributions are appreciated,” and you drop a quarter in the box. When a poet makes his poetry—like nowadays you buy tickets for a concert—the poet just came up with long poetry. It took a lot of work, and at the big bonfire poetry concert, people throw money at him. That’s how he makes his living.

The Prophet ﷺ is essentially saying: You called me a poet. Have I ever asked you for money? The soothsayer, the magician—they ask for money. When have I ever done that? When have I ever asked you for anything?

إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ أَن يَتَّخِذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا—the only thing I ask you is: whoever wants to take a path toward his Master, a journey that ends with Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 58

وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْحَىِّ ٱلَّذِى لَا يَمُوتُ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِۦ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًا

Continue to place your trust upon the Living, the One who doesn’t die.

Your Master is living. He doesn’t die. The Sahaba (RA) around you will die. You thought they were so important, you couldn’t do without them, but they will be tortured and killed. You will lose people along the way, and you will be demoralized by it. But you need to place your reliance and trust upon the One who lives and won’t die.

وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِۦ—and continue to declare His perfection. When you hit the worst kinds of calamities, learn to thank Allah ﷻ. Learn to thank Allah ﷻ that He has given you this Quran and this honor.

وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًا—and Allah ﷻ Himself is enough to have full news of what some of His slaves have done. He’s fully knowledgeable of the shameful or disappointing acts of His slaves.

When we study the Quran, it’s really a study of the life of the Prophet ﷺ. It’s Allah ﷻ talking to him in real time. We’re getting an insider’s view of the seerah. The seerah book is the outsider’s view—the writer is telling you this happened, this happened, this happened. The Quran is different. Allah ﷻ says to the Messenger ﷺ, “Listen, this just happened. Let me tell you how to deal with it.” It’s the inside view of the seerah.

That’s why the study of the seerah is really incomplete without the Quran, and the study of the Quran is really incomplete without the seerah. They need one another. Those two studies need each other, because the Quran was delivered through the seerah. The seerah is the delivery of the Quran.

Ayah 59

ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَسْـَٔلْ بِهِۦ خَبِيرًا

The One who created the skies and the earth and whatever lies between the both of them in six days, then He rose upon the Throne.

Ar-Rahman. Ask about Him to someone who knows, someone more informed.

The discussion of Allah’s ﷻ rising upon the Throne—what that literally means is beyond our understanding, and that’s perfectly fine. We don’t have to get into a discussion about it. Knowing exactly what it looks like, whether there’s an actual throne, what it means for Allah ﷻ to be upon it—these questions suggest that figuring that out has something to do with your salvation, and that the entire conversation going on in this surah is somehow not as important as figuring out what the Arsh looks like. This trivializes the religion and reduces it to things that don’t actually matter to Allah ﷻ Himself.

But why mention it then? Allah ﷻ mentions these things to show us His grandeur, to show us His greatness, and to help us understand that nothing can be compared to Allah ﷻ.

When normal kings rise on the throne, it’s the time when they start executing punishments against those they couldn’t touch before. When they weren’t on the throne, they hated some people but couldn’t do anything. The moment they seize the throne, they start: this one is to be killed, this one is to be arrested. In the history of the Israelites, there were different kinds of kings who were good to them. When the pharaohs took over, they came after the Israelites and turned them into slaves.

Allah ﷻ says He rose upon the Throne, but what kind of Master, what kind of King is He? الرَّحْمَـٰنُ—the excessively merciful. His kingdom is defined by mercy instead of punishment.

From the very beginning of creation, Allah ﷻ did not describe Himself as someone who is out to punish. He described Himself as someone who is out to show mercy.

فَسْـَٔلْ بِهِۦ خَبِيرًا—then ask about Him to someone who knows, ask about Him to someone who is more informed than you are.

This also connects to what comes next: they hated the name Ar-Rahman.

Ayah 60

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ قَالُوا۟ وَمَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ أَنَسْجُدُ لِمَا تَأْمُرُنَا وَزَادَهُمْ نُفُورًا ۩

When they are told, “Make sajdah”—you know, when a king takes his throne, you’re supposed to show acknowledgment to the king. “All hail the king!” What’s the acknowledgment of our King? Sajdah.

When they were told, “Bow before this King in frustration,” they say, “What’s Rahman? I don’t know what you’re talking about.”

أَنَسْجُدُ لِمَا تَأْمُرُنَا—should we make sajdah to someone you suggest, you told us about, or you told us to?

This is a very important psychological concept in this small phrase: مَا تَأْمُرُنَا.

Let me give you an example closer to home. You have a little brother or a big sister you can’t stand. She tells you, “Hey, you should try that cookie.” “No, I don’t want it.
