If you told me to get it, I don’t want it. Don’t want nothing to do with it.” Your real reason for not wanting it is because she said it’s a good idea. You ever have that experience? “I’m not going to do it—not because it’s a bad thing, but because they said I should do it. Because if I do it now, it’ll be like I listened to you, like you’re important or something. I really wanted that cookie too. I wish you didn’t tell me to eat it. Now I can’t eat it.”

Siblings do this when they play with each other. My older daughter is really clever. She wants to sit in the window seat in the car, so she’ll tell her younger sister, “Hey, sit in the window seat.” “Okay, fine, I’ll go sit there.” Five minutes later, the younger will be like, “Mama, I never got to sit in the window.” The older will be like, “Hey, I asked you if you could sit there.” “I said no, because I can’t be seen as obeying you. Not because I don’t want to.”

This is very real. It’s a psychological thing people have with those close to them. You don’t want to be seen as listening to somebody else. That’s why when you try to give religious advice to your family—“You’re going to tell me? Go give a lecture elsewhere, don’t talk to me.” If they will listen, it’s when you’re not watching. Maybe they will do what you told them, but they cannot give you the satisfaction of knowing they did it. There can’t be a visible cause and effect—like they did it because you said it. If that “because” is there, they don’t want that credit to go to you, because you might use it the next day: “I told you to do it and you did it, right? You listened to me, right?” That’s going to burn for a while. Can’t do it.

This kind of pride isn’t just between siblings. This becomes between nations, between people and the Messenger ﷺ.

“We should do sajdah to the one you said we should do sajdah to? I was even thinking about it, but now that you’re saying it? No. Not going to happen.”

وَزَادَهُمْ نُفُورًا—and it increases them even more in their aggression. They get into battle mode now. نُفُور means platoons of warriors. They turn into warriors. They turn aggressive. They don’t want to hear it.

The one who leaves home for battle—he’s out to fight. You see that guy walking, you know he’s going for a fight. Like if you see someone wearing boxing gloves or carrying a gun or a sword, walking around ready—this guy’s aggressive, he’s out looking for trouble.

Allah ﷻ is telling us: they say this and it makes them super aggressive. It makes them full of hatred and aggression.

This is the ayah of sajdah (۩), a good point to stop. The contrast Allah ﷻ wants to show: we just talked about the most arrogant people, really arrogant people full of themselves. They can’t let their pride be undermined by giving the Messenger ﷺ the satisfaction of knowing they listened to him. Sajdah is the last thing an arrogant person can do.

The contrast will be shown in the next passage. What do people who are truly humbled to Ar-Rahman look like? What is their personality like? Two surahs ago in Surah Mu’minun, we saw the character of the true believer. Now we’re going to see a special breed of true believers that are so close to Allah ﷻ that He calls them عِبَادُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ—it’s a special title Allah ﷻ gave these people. He will describe their qualities in the passage that follows.

Ayah 61–77 · linked video: 03. Al-Furqan (Ayah 61-77)

Ayah 61

تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ بُرُوجًا وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا سِرَٰجًا وَقَمَرًا مُّنِيرًا

We arrive at ayah 61 of Surah Al-Furqan, the beginning of the final passage. This passage opens with تَبَارَكَ, the same blessed word that initiated the entire surah. “How blessed is the One who placed in the sky بُرُوجًا - shining towers or brilliant stars.” The word بُرُوج can be understood as tall, magnificent towers or as constellations of brilliant stars. The root ب-ر-ج relates to beauty and prominence, suggesting something elevated and striking.

Allah ﷻ then mentions placing in the sky سِرَاجًا - a lamp, referring to the sun - and قَمَرًا مُّنِيرًا - a moon that gives off light. The word مُنِير indicates the moon’s reflective quality, deflecting light rather than producing it.

Ayah 62

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ خِلْفَةً لِّمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرَادَ شُكُورًا

Allah ﷻ is the One who made the night and day خِلْفَةً - in conflict with one another, alternating back and forth. This alternation serves two types of people. First, لِّمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَذَّكَّرَ - for those who want to make an effort to remember. The nighttime arrives and it’s time for night prayer. Morning comes and it’s time for morning prayer. Afternoon arrives and it’s time for afternoon prayer. The prayers of the believer revolve around this constant battle between night and day.

Second, أَوْ أَرَادَ شُكُورًا - for those who want to be grateful. Every time a full day completes or they make it through the night, they turn back to Allah ﷻ and thank Him. The slave thanks Allah ﷻ for another day they were allowed to breathe on this earth, another opportunity to serve Him and earn His forgiveness.

Ayah 63

وَعِبَادُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا وَإِذَا خَاطَبَهُمُ ٱلْجَـٰهِلُونَ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا

Now we see a shift in focus. The surah began with the greatness of Allah ﷻ, contrasted with the false greatness of those who are arrogant and refuse to accept His message. Here we learn that the one who believes in Allah ﷻ - the more they realize that life is great, the more they realize they themselves are not. The greatness of Allah ﷻ necessarily leads to one’s humility. The phrase “Allahu Akbar” is a declaration of two things: Allah’s greatness and my lack thereof.

وَعِبَادُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ - the slaves of the Ultimately Merciful. These are people who walk on earth هَوْنًا - with humility, with weakness, with softness. They don’t stomp on the earth when they walk. In Surah Al-Isra (17:37), Allah ﷻ told us, “Don’t walk on the earth full of yourself. You’re not going to cause cracks in the earth when you walk.”

This isn’t just about literal walking. Some people have a swagger in their walk, a way of moving that intimidates others or displays pride. Their walk itself makes people uncomfortable or fearful. This applies to both young men and young women. The believer walks with humility and composure.

The ayah continues: وَإِذَا خَاطَبَهُمُ ٱلْجَـٰهِلُونَ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا - when the ignorant address them, they say “peace.” The word جَاهِل refers to someone who doesn’t have control over their emotions. The word خَاطَبَ means to address someone out loud, publicly. From this root we get خِطْبَة - a public declaration, including marriage proposals, which are meant to be public announcements.

Ignorant people don’t lower their voices. They want attention. They’re trying to impress their friends, saying the most outrageous things to prove how “crazy” they are. They feed off each other’s reactions, competing to say increasingly obscene or shocking things. They have no limits, no boundaries on what they’ll say.

When such people engage you, the best response is سَلَامًا - peace. This doesn’t necessarily mean saying “As-salamu alaikum.” It could mean diffusing the situation: “It’s okay, just calm down.” It means giving them peace from you - no conflict coming from your side. You’re not going to engage further.

This is crucial wisdom: when someone is being ignorant and emotional, you cannot engage them in intellectual conversation. You cannot present rational proofs to someone who has abandoned reason for raw emotion. The opposite of جَاهِل is عَاقِل - someone who controls their emotions and uses their mind instead.

We can also understand جَاهِل in a less aggressive sense - someone who is emotionally overwhelmed, extremely depressed, or in a terrible mood. Your family member might be going through something difficult. Your friend might be struggling. There’s a time to bring up certain conversations and a time to leave things alone. When someone is in a bad emotional state, it’s going to start trouble. You need the intelligence and humility to recognize that and hold back.

Between husband and wife, this sensitivity is essential. The husband comes home and can see from his wife’s face what kind of day she’s had. The way she says “salaam” tells him everything. If you truly know your spouse, you recognize when they’re emotionally volatile. One wrong word and everything explodes. “Do you want salt?” “What did I say about salt?” The believer has this kind of emotional intelligence. They’re sensitive to people’s states before addressing them.

Sometimes this patience is severely tested. At conventions, someone might approach you with an urgent question while you’re trying to calm a crying baby. They want answers right now, unable to wait even a moment. This is difficult because people who come seeking knowledge are an amanah - a trust. They’ve come to you thinking you might have something to offer that will bring them closer to Allah ﷻ. You can’t simply dismiss them.

The Prophet ﷺ was once speaking with an important member of the tribe when Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum (RA), who was blind, came seeking knowledge. The Prophet ﷺ frowned, frustrated by the interruption. Allah ﷻ revealed Surah ’Abasa in response: “He frowned…” The question arises: if Ibn Umm Maktum (RA) was blind, how could he be offended? Allah ﷻ was offended. The lesson is that you shouldn’t get frustrated. You must be patient with people.

The more you engage in this work - the more you’re involved in da’wah, teaching, helping at Sunday school - the more your patience will be tested. It becomes increasingly difficult. For younger believers who will be active in da’wah, understand this: once you take any kind of religious position, once you’re the one people turn to with questions, everything you do represents Islam. You no longer have a purely personal life. Everything you do will be labeled, and one mistake can wash away everything you’ve said before.

All human beings make big mistakes. But when you’re in da’wah, people are looking for ways to dismiss your message. One misstep and they’ll say, “See? He made that mistake.” You have to be extra cautious, holding yourself to a much higher standard.

Ayah 64

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَـٰمًا

After describing how these slaves of Ar-Rahman engage with ignorant people - leaving them in peace - the ayah shows what gives them strength to handle difficult conversations with maturity: their private conversation with Allah ﷻ.

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَـٰمًا - these are the people who spend the night before their Master in prostration and standing. Notice that سُجَّدًا - prostration - is mentioned first because this passage highlights humility. What is the most humble position in prayer? Sujud.

As you’ll see at the end of this passage, one of the best positions for making du’a is in sujud. So they’re in prostration and they’re standing - سُجَّدًا وَقِيَـٰمًا - engaged in prayer during the depths of night.

Ayah 65

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذَابَ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ إِنَّ عَذَابَهَا كَانَ غَرَامًا

As they’re in sujud and qiyam, praying in the night, they’re making du’a: رَبَّنَا ٱصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذَابَ جَهَنَّمَ - “Our Master, remove from us, ward off from us the punishment of Hellfire.”

The word ٱصْرِفْ means to alter, to change the course of something. They’re asking Allah ﷻ to change the trajectory of any punishment that might be headed their way, to put it on a different track so it doesn’t strike them.

إِنَّ عَذَابَهَا كَانَ غَرَامًا - “Indeed, its punishment has always been one that sticks.” The word غَرَام means something that clings and doesn’t let go. It also means a huge loss or penalty. The punishment of Hellfire is both devastating and inescapable.

Ayah 66

إِنَّهَا سَآءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا

إِنَّهَا سَآءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا - “It is a terrible place to be, even for a little while (mustaqarr) and as a permanent residence (muqam).”

The word مُسْتَقَرّ refers to a place where you stop briefly for rest. In the desert, when an Arab traveler finds a tree, he rushes to it for shade, sits down for a moment to escape the heat, then continues his journey. That brief stopping place is a mustaqarr - like a rest stop, a lobby, a waiting area at an airport gate where people hang out temporarily.

The believer says to Allah ﷻ: “Jahannam is a horrible place to be even if I’m there for a few minutes, a few seconds.” وَمُقَامًا - and of course, as a permanent residence where one remains forever.

This is crucial because some Muslims think, “Yeah, I messed up, but Allah ﷻ won’t send me to Hell forever. I’ll just go for like a weekend, maybe a couple days, then He’ll let me go because I’m Muslim after all. I fasted in Ramadan. I should be alright.” This is the delusion that Allah ﷻ addresses here. The real believer says: “I don’t want to be there at all - not for a little bit, not for a long time.”

This echoes what we discussed in Surah Al-Mu’minun, where being touched by Hellfire even for a split second is unbearable. Whether for a moment or an eternity, it’s a horrible place. The believers in this ayah are asking to be completely spared from it.

Allah ﷻ knew that centuries later, there would be confused, deluded Muslims asking these kinds of absurd questions: “What if I only go for a little bit? It shouldn’t be that bad, right?” This is similar to what some Jews had said: “The Fire will not touch us except for a few numbered days.” Allah ﷻ addresses this false sense of security directly.

Ayah 67

وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا۟ وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ قَوَامًا

The night is over and we’re back in the day. After describing the spiritual life of these great slaves of Ar-Rahman, Allah ﷻ now discusses their shopping habits - how they spend money.

وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا۟ - when they spend, they don’t go overboard. The word إِسْرَاف means extravagance, spending too much on something. If it’s overpriced, they recognize it: “That’s too expensive. I can afford it, but I know it’s overpriced. I’m not going to buy that. I’ll find a better deal somewhere.”

You could buy the same thing cheaper elsewhere, but you want to shop at that department store so you can walk away with the bag that makes you look like you bought expensive stuff. That’s israf. It also means spending more than you should on things that are legitimate in themselves. Clothing is legitimate, but if you spend excessively on it, that’s israf. Food is legitimate, but if you go to a restaurant that’s $100 per plate, that’s israf.

وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا۟ - and they don’t hold back. The root ق-ت-ر means to shrink or constrict. قَتَر means to hold back the family budget literally, refusing to spend even on necessities. The word قَتُور describes someone who refuses to spend even though he has the money, even on essentials.

Imagine going grocery shopping with your spouse. Every time you put a cereal box in the cart: “Do we have to get that? Do we need all 12 eggs?” You’re pinching pennies to an extreme degree, becoming overly frugal. This is قُتُور.

وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ قَوَامًا - they find a way to stand and remain balanced between those two extremes. They’re not cheap and obsessive with their budget, but they’re not carefree either, saying “Get it! It’s on me!” at every opportunity. They maintain قَوَام - balance, standing firm between the two extremes.

Ayah 68

وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ وَلَا يَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَزْنُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثَامًا

After mentioning their personal financial habits, Allah ﷻ now addresses the bigger picture: وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ - these are people who don’t call upon anyone besides Allah ﷻ in any way, shape or form.

وَلَا يَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ - they stay away from the major sins. They don’t kill anyone - they would never think of engaging in the crime of murder.

A question arises: why is Allah ﷻ mentioning this to people who stand in prayer at night? Are they really at risk of committing murder? What Allah ﷻ is teaching us is that goodness toward Allah ﷻ starts with taking care of the major sins first. Yes, there are minute details and ways to excel spiritually, but that doesn’t make you above the law. Spirituality doesn’t exempt you from basic religious obligations.

This has been a problem in Islamic history. Some people, because they felt they had reached a certain spiritual level, thought “I don’t have to attend the congregational prayer. I’m doing my personal dhikr.” There’s a famous statement where someone said, “I was right at the footsteps of the King’s throne” - meaning intimate with Allah ﷻ through personal worship - “and now I’m being called to go outside the gates.” He felt that joining the congregation ruined his personal spiritual connection, as though he was being distanced from Allah ﷻ.

This kind of thinking suggests that congregational prayer is beneath your spiritual level. But the Quran teaches us: no matter how high you become spiritually, you don’t become above the law that Allah ﷻ has revealed. You excel above and beyond that standard, but the standard itself is perfection.

وَلَا يَزْنُونَ - and they don’t commit zina (fornication/adultery). Two capital crimes are mentioned here: murder and zina. In Surah Al-Isra (17:32), the order is reversed: “Don’t even go near zina… and don’t kill…” Here it’s “don’t kill” then “don’t commit zina.”

The interchangeability of these two crimes teaches us something powerful: these are equally offensive to Allah ﷻ after shirk. One kills the body, the other kills the soul. Both are forms of murder. Zina is spiritual murder. Allah ﷻ wants us to maintain ourselves both physically and spiritually.

وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثَامًا - whoever has done that has come face to face with, has met with, a really serious sin. The word إِثْم means a fault, doing less than what was expected, falling short of doing the right thing. They’ve made a grave mistake.

Ayah 69

يُضَـٰعَفْ لَهُ ٱلْعَذَابُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِۦ مُهَانًا

يُضَـٰعَفْ لَهُ ٱلْعَذَابُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - the punishment will be doubled for that type of person on the Day of Resurrection. It will be multiplied for them.

Some scholars have used this ayah to argue for the punishment of the grave, and I agree with that position. If the punishment is being multiplied on Judgment Day, it implies it already existed before Judgment Day in some form, then was multiplied. The ayah thus alludes to the punishment of the grave that is then intensified on the Day of Judgment.

وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِۦ مُهَانًا - and he will remain in it, humiliated. The word مُهَان comes from مَهِين - humiliated, disgraced.

Why is the punishment doubled? Allah ﷻ tells us: First, the crime committed is that of shirk - they already knew Allah ﷻ and still committed a crime against Him. Second, they committed a crime against another human being. So the punishment is doubled - once for the crime against Allah ﷻ, and once for the crime against His creation.

Ayah 70

إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ حَسَنَـٰتٍ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا

This is one of the most amazing ayat of forgiveness in the Quran. إِلَّا مَن تَابَ - except for the one who repented to Allah ﷻ, who turned back.

Let’s be clear about what crimes have been mentioned: shirk, murder, and zina. These aren’t small matters. These are serious, major crimes. Yet Allah ﷻ says: even from those people, if someone makes tawbah…

وَءَامَنَ- and comes back to iman. When you make tawbah properly, it’s like you became Muslim all over again. You’re born again as a Muslim. What do we tell someone who just became Muslim? All previous sins are forgiven. This person who makes real tawbah has started life over, clean as when he was first born.

وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا - and did righteous action. Now that he’s revived himself, corrected his way, straightened out, every action he does from now on is a good action. He’s changed his behavior completely.

This is a three-step process: 1. He made tawbah 2. He revived his faith - came back to Islam again
3. As a result, from now on he behaves properly

If these three can be accomplished, here’s what Allah ﷻ will do:

فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ حَسَنَـٰتٍ - then those people, Allah ﷻ will replace their evil sins with good deeds.

Imagine someone committed murder - a very serious sin. His punishment in this dunya is already revealed. We know the worldly punishment for murder exists. But that same person who is being punished in this world - if he makes tawbah, if he truly repents - in the akhirah, that crime of murder will be transformed. Imagine a mountain-sized sin. Allah ﷻ will take that mountain and convert it into a mountain’s worth of good deeds for him in the akhirah.

This is crucial to understand: worldly punishments remain worldly punishments. Human judgment remains human judgment. But Allah’s judgment is something else entirely. Allah ﷻ revealed ways by which we judge human beings here, but just as this world is limited, that judgment doesn’t extend to the next life’s consequences.

For example, if someone stole, what’s the punishment? Cutting the hand. They walk around society with a cut hand - people can see it, they’re being judged by society. But actually, we have no right to judge beyond applying the revealed punishment. The punishment has been revealed, but the right to judge the person’s worth hasn’t been revealed. If that person made tawbah, then with Allah ﷻ, that crime of stealing is turned into credit for them. That’s spiritual judgment - the judgment of the unseen world.

Consider the woman who committed zina and came forward to the Prophet ﷺ, begging to be punished. She begged to be punished, and she was. When she was executed, the Prophet ﷺ said her tawbah could get the entire city of Madinah forgiven. Her tawbah was that powerful. The punishment was still delivered in this world, but her judgment with Allah ﷻ was a separate matter.

This teaches us: when people have made huge mistakes and they make tawbah, don’t judge them. You don’t know - if they were that deep down in the hole before, maybe now they’re that high up in the sky as far as Allah ﷻ is concerned. You simply don’t know.

In our religion, it’s not even a clean slate. Your previous sins aren’t just forgiven - if you really made tawbah from those sins, those sins have been turned into good deeds. You’re way ahead of everybody else now.

وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا - and Allah ﷻ has always been extremely forgiving, always merciful.

Ayah 71

وَمَن تَابَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَإِنَّهُۥ يَتُوبُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَتَابًا

وَمَن تَابَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا - whoever did repent and did in fact act righteously. Now that we’ve understood that tawbah means becoming a believer again, reviving your faith, it doesn’t need to be mentioned again. You’re told the direct consequences: two things - make tawbah and get your act together. Don’t do that stuff again.

فَإِنَّهُۥ يَتُوبُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَتَابًا - then he has truly, truly turned to Allah ﷻ in a way that’s very serious and significant. It’s not just تَوْبَة, it’s مَتَاب- a strong, emphatic form of tawbah.

What Allah ﷻ is telling us: the one who makes tawbah and it shows in their behavior - that’s what I call tawbah. Someone says “I’m sorry,” they beg, they cry, they say they won’t do it again - fine, that’s tawbah. But then next week they’re up to the same thing. That’s not real tawbah.

Allah ﷻ says in Surah An-Nisa (4:17-18): “Tawbah is not for those people who keep doing sins until death comes, and then say ‘Okay, I’m not going to do it again.’” Real tawbah is for people who truly repent and then change their behavior. That person has done tawbah to Allah ﷻ - that’s real tawbah.

Allah ﷻ compliments this tawbah so much, He uses the strongest term for it. There are three words for repentance in Arabic: إِنَابَة, تَوْبَة, and مَتَاب. All three are used in the Quran. Matab is the strongest of the three. Allah ﷻ is saying: because this person showed change in behavior, that is the most amazing form of tawbah. That’s what you call true repentance.

Ayah 72

وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَشْهَدُونَ ٱلزُّورَ وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِٱللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا۟ كِرَامًا

وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَشْهَدُونَ ٱلزُّورَ - these are people who don’t witness false testimony. This means a couple of things. First, they don’t give false testimony. But it also means they don’t even like to be witness to falsehood taking place. They don’t want anything to do with it. They won’t sit around watching wrong things happen. They won’t just sit there and let injustice go by while they watch. They will take no part in it. They will protest. They will walk out.

I’m reminded of a profound hadith where Allah ﷻ told the angels to destroy a town. They said, “In this town there’s a slave of Yours - not a blink of an eye has gone by that he hasn’t worshiped You. You want us to crush that town?” Allah ﷻ said, “Start with him. Turn the town over on him, crush him first, because his blood didn’t boil when injustice was done, when wrong was spread. It didn’t bother him.” He was content keeping his religion to himself, thinking “At least I’m good.”

This passage began with people deeply spiritual in the middle of the night, asking Allah ﷻ to save them from punishment. Now we’re learning these same people can’t stand falsehood going on around them. They can’t experience evil and remain passive.

وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِٱللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا۟ كِرَامًا - and on top of that, if they even pass by useless conversation, useless company, a useless gathering - people are backbiting, doing inappropriate things, engaging in useless activities - they don’t stay there. They make a dignified escape: “I’m so sorry, I have to get going.” “Can’t you stay?” “I really can’t. I’m really sorry. Thank you so much for the invitation. InshaAllah I’ll see you next time.”

مَرُّوا۟ كِرَامًا - they pass by it in a dignified way. It also means they don’t get involved in it, because those who get involved end up losing their dignity. They start saying indecent things. The opposite of this we read in Surah An-Nur: people staying up all night, hanging out, saying inappropriate things - the later the night gets, the more inappropriate they become.

These believers make a dignified escape. You can use this opportunity to inspire others: “It’s getting late. I won’t get up for Fajr if I don’t go now.” What does that do? Others might think, “If he’s worried about Fajr, maybe I should worry about that too.”

Ayah 73

وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَمْ يَخِرُّوا۟ عَلَيْهَا صُمًّا وَعُمْيَانًا

This is one of my favorite ayat in the Quran. وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ - when they are reminded of the ayat of their Master - they’re listening to a khutbah, listening to Quran, listening to someone explain the Quran, being reminded of the Quran.

لَمْ يَخِرُّوا۟ عَلَيْهَا - they don’t fall over them, trip over them, stumble on them. The word خَرَّ means to stumble. Imagine walking and not realizing there’s a log in front of you. You kick it hard, stumble, and keep going, thinking “I don’t know what that was, but it sure got in my way.” That’s the attitude some have toward the Quran - “I don’t care what that was, but it made me uncomfortable. I stumbled when I heard it.”

These believers don’t stumble. In what state do they not stumble? صُمًّا وَعُمْيَانًا - deaf and blind.

Deaf is easier to understand - they’re not the kind of people who don’t pay attention to the ayat being recited, who tune it out, zone out, or don’t really focus. That’s one level. They focus when ayat are being discussed.

But what about blind? How does being blind relate to listening to ayat? When someone can see, they see the visible world. But when they hear the ayat, they should get a picture of the unseen reality. The unseen reality should change the way you look at the seen reality. If it hasn’t changed the way you perceive the visible world, you’re still blind. You still haven’t changed your worldview. You’re still blind.

These believers don’t pass over the ayat without their perceptions changing, without their attitudes transforming. If you truly tune in, the ayat will change your outlook. If you tune out, nothing changes. My teacher used to describe people who’ve been listening so long they’ve become numb: they hear but nothing registers anymore.

Ayah 74

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا هَبْ لَنَا مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِنَا وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِنَا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ وَٱجْعَلْنَا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِمَامًا

وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا هَبْ لَنَا مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِنَا وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِنَا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ- these are the kinds of people who say “Our Master, give us the gift - give us a gift from ourselves, out of our spouses and out of our future generations, our children, our grandchildren.”

These people are thinking about their married life and about not just their children but their grandchildren and their grandchildren’s children. They’re asking: مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِنَا وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِنَا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ - “Give us coolness of the eyes from our spouses and descendants.”

The phrase قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ means coolness of the eyes, calm, peace, something that brings such joy it makes you tear up with happiness. قُرَّة comes from قَرَّ - when your eyes water from joy. Make us so happy with our spouses, so happy with our kids, that it brings tears of joy. Make us love them so much, care for them so much that we can’t help but cry from happiness.

And more - قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ also means “I can’t take my eyes off them.” Few things give you the kind of joy more than watching your child recite Quran, watching your child pray on their own without being told. You’re working in the corner and from the corner of your eye you see your daughter stand up, make wudu, and pray on her own. That will make you cry. When your child does that independently, it brings tears of joy.

Otherwise children cry when you tell them it’s prayer time - that’s not the kind of tears we’re talking about. The joy of seeing them embrace the deen willingly - that’s what brings tears of joy.

وَٱجْعَلْنَا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِمَامًا - “and make us a leader, an imam for the people who have taqwa.”

This last phrase implies something beautiful: everybody in the household becomes an imam. Everybody. If you have somebody under you, you’re an imam. Every husband is an imam, every father is an imam. Your congregation is your household. The imam makes sure prayers are established, and the imam is responsible for his congregation on the Day of Judgment.

I won’t be standing alone on Judgment Day. My immediate congregation - my wife and kids - are standing behind me, and I’m being asked about their behavior first. The first person asked on Judgment Day is the messenger. Look at what ’Isa (AS) is asked in Surah Al-Ma’idah (5:116): “Did you say to people ‘Take me and my mother as gods besides Allah ﷻ?’” The first one questioned is the messenger, the imam.

The Prophet ﷺ understood this profoundly. He asked the Companions at Hajjatul-Wada: “Have I delivered the message?” They all responded “Yes!” He made them witness because he knew he would be asked. He got it on official record: “I did my job. Allah ﷻ, be witness - they said I did my job.”

We will all be asked on Judgment Day about those under our care. If you didn’t care about raising your kids properly, if it wasn’t a big deal to you, if you were indifferent, if you just didn’t know what to do and left it on the back burner - you cannot raise kids on the back burner in America. It’s not possible. They won’t come out right.

Actually, you can’t be on autopilot anywhere in the world now, after the information explosion. Just recently, I was at tarawih with my family. After I prayed, I came out and let my wife go pray. We were watching the kids in the van, some had fallen asleep. While she was praying, two 12 or 13-year-old kids were sitting next to each other in the parking lot. She could hear their conversation. On the way back, she was crying.

The things these kids were talking about - she’d never heard such things in her life. The mildest of which I’ll tell you: one was teaching the other how to create a subfolder on his mobile device that his parents can’t find, how to create another Facebook profile his parents can’t check. And that was the least disturbing part of their conversation - these 12 and 13-year-olds.

These parents brought their kids to the masjid. They’re not bad parents - they’re trying, they’re praying, they think bringing kids to the masjid will be good for them. But those are our kids, your kids and mine. These are Muslim kids. We have to take collective ownership.

Even bringing them to the masjid is autopilot now. We have to be more intentional. We can’t just get قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ automatically. It’s going to take serious work. We need to know our kids personally - each one of them - who their best friends are, what they like, what they don’t like, what shows they watch. We need to talk to them as friends, not just as authorities. The authoritarian approach won’t work anymore. Kids will just develop secret lives, multiple personalities. It’s so easy now.

This isn’t just for parents. I’m talking to older brothers and sisters too. You know much more than your eight-year-old sibling. You know what’s ahead of them. Act as a guardian too. You don’t have to spell everything out to your parents if they won’t understand - that’s okay. But you should take that active role. Be the counselor. Care deeply.

Forget this immature culture of “I hate my little brother, don’t hang around me, this is my room, get out.” That’s destructive. Sibling rivalry can ruin households. Parents may not fully understand - they came from a different continent. There’s not just a generation gap, there’s a continental gap. Two kinds of gaps. It’s understandable they won’t get everything. If you understand better, help them with your siblings.

We’re going to be raised together and we’ll be asked. They’re chained to us on Judgment Day. If they’re going down, they’re dragging us with them. This is the weight of وَٱجْعَلْنَا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِمَامًا - making us imams for the people of taqwa, starting with our own households.

Ayah 75

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ ٱلْغُرْفَةَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ وَيُلَقَّوْنَ فِيهَا تَحِيَّةً وَسَلَـٰمًا

Here’s where you appreciate the difference between modern Arabic and classical Arabic. أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ ٱلْغُرْفَةَ - “Those are the ones who will be compensated with al-ghurfah.”

In modern Arabic, غُرْفَة means “the room.” If you tried reading this with modern Arabic, you’d think “They’ll be compensated with the room - like a studio, 300 square feet. Congratulations on your Jannah apartment!”

But الْغُرْفَة in classical Arabic means something tall, high-ceilinged, lofty - a vast space. They’ll walk in and say “Whoa! All of this is mine? I thought it was my apartment in this building somewhere - this whole thing is mine?!”

بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ - because of the patience they displayed. All those qualities mentioned - their humility, their night prayers, their patience with ignorant people, their financial balance, their avoidance of major sins, their genuine tawbah, their refusal to witness falsehood, their attention to the ayat, their care for their families - all of this required صَبْر, patience and perseverance.

وَيُلَقَّوْنَ فِيهَا تَحِيَّةً وَسَلَـٰمًا - and they will be greeted constantly with greetings and peace. Everywhere they go, people will know them: “Hey! Salam! How are you doing? Oh, that’s you over there? Cool, yeah, mine’s over here. It’s pretty awesome. We should hang out sometime!”

Everywhere they go, they’re being greeted. Who do you greet and say salam to? People you like and know. You have to know them first. In Jannah, we’ll know people across generations. When you and I get to Jannah, we’ll know our great-great-great-great-grandfather who became Muslim - that guy. We’ll know the Sahaba (RA). We’ll know the great heroes of Islam, the great scholars. We’ll know the person nobody else knew - the man who used to clean the masjid, who opened its doors for Fajr and nobody knew his name. We’ll know him personally.

SubhanAllah! The best people of the world - we’ll know all of them. In this world, meeting good people, knowing good people, getting time with them is so difficult. Everyone has obligations and responsibilities. “I wish I could spend more time with him, with her” - it’s very hard. In Jannah, we’ll all have time for each other, greeting one another constantly.

Ayah 76

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا - they will remain in it permanently. حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا - what a beautiful place to be, for a little bit or permanently.

What does “a little bit” mean in Jannah? They’re in one palace temporarily because Allah ﷻ says “Actually, I have another palace for you. Come, let me show you another one.” So you were there as a mustaqarr - staying temporarily - and then you move to something even better. Enhancement upon enhancement upon enhancement - and all of it is permanent.

In previous Quran studies, we’ve learned that your family member, your cousin, might have made it to the seventh level of Paradise while you’re on the ground floor, the first level. To appreciate the distance between levels, the Prophet ﷺ described it like us looking at the stars - that’s one level to the next. It’s incredibly high.

But here’s the mercy of Allah ﷻ: if one of your family members made it to floor seven and you’re on floor one, you get a guest pass. You move into floor seven with them. You go up - they don’t have to come down. He doesn’t have to leave his reserved elite platinum seat to go down to the bleachers with the rest of your family. No - Jannah is awesome by itself, but if one family member is up there and you’re down here, you get to go up there. You move in over there. Connections!

Some people confuse this to mean if you have a family member in Paradise and you’re in Hellfire, they’ll pull you out. No, this is between Paradise levels only. This doesn’t pull someone out of Hellfire into Paradise.

Ayah 77

قُلْ مَا يَعْبَؤُا۟ بِكُمْ رَبِّى لَوْلَا دُعَآؤُكُمْ ۖ فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًۢا

The last ayah turns back to the mushrikin. This surah described the humble believers and the beautiful life they will have. Now it addresses the arrogant:

قُلْ مَا يَعْبَؤُا۟ بِكُمْ رَبِّى - “Say: My Master doesn’t care about you.” The word يَعْبَأ means to value, to give weight to. “What burden are you? My Master doesn’t value you. He doesn’t give you any weight. Who do you think you are?”

The arrogance they’ve shown, how important they think they are - “You people are nothing to my Master.” The Quraysh’s argument was: “If we have all this authority, power, control in Makkah, it must mean Allah ﷻ cares about us. That’s why He gave us all this wealth and influence.”

Allah ﷻ is telling them: “Get it through your heads - Allah ﷻ doesn’t care about people like you. When you stop caring about Him, He values you as nothing. None whatsoever. Don’t even entertain the thought that He cares about you or will take care of you in some special way.”

لَوْلَا دُعَآؤُكُمْ - “had it not been for the du’a made on your behalf,” meaning the prayers the Messenger ﷺ is making for you, calling you to guidance. Don’t think that just because the Messenger ﷺ is calling you, it means we’re desperate to get you to become Muslim. That’s only for your own benefit.

فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ - “Then you’ve already lied against it and proven you’re not worthy of him.”

فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًۢا - “Soon this accusation, this disbelief of yours, this lying of yours - it’s going to stick. It will be slapped onto you and it won’t come off.”

The word لِزَام means something that clings permanently. Right now you’re committed to lying against this message, but soon this will become your permanent state. I’m still giving you a little bit of room, a little bit of time. But soon you will be judged as such. Soon this will become permanently sealed. Watch out - you’re treading very dangerous waters.

Don’t think Allah ﷻ cares especially about you even though you’ve acted this way. This is one reason people act arrogantly - they think they have a free pass. Like a child whose mother loves him so much that he starts getting an attitude, becomes more and more aggressive. She keeps showing mercy and he uses that mercy to become even more obnoxious. Until one day the mother says “Get out of my house.” And the child is shocked: “What? You mean it?” “Yes, get out. It’s too late now.”

Allah ﷻ is showing mercy, and the mushrikin are using that mercy to become more obnoxious. The warning is clear: Watch it. Allah ﷻ might make this your permanent psychological state, and then you will absolutely be burning in Hell.

فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًۢا - “Soon that’s going to be the case.” Why “soon”? Because this is middle Makkah. They still have a few more years before the deal is sealed and they’re called كَافِر - done. All chances that could be given have been given.

A point of reflection here: Prophet Nuh (AS) delivered his message for 950 years, and after all that time, Allah ﷻ said their hearts are sealed. The Messenger ﷺ delivered his message for 13 years in Makkah, and Allah ﷻ says enough time has passed, their hearts are sealed.

How can what took 950 years be compared to just 13 years? This should make us appreciate the power of the Quran. The Quran did in 13 years what without the Quran took 950 years. The convincing that had to be done over nearly a millennium was accomplished in just over a decade through the Quran’s eloquence, perfection, clarity and penetrating truth.

There’s another reason: how many times is a miracle shown? Once, twice, a few times. But how many miracles are in the Quran? As many as there are ayat. They were being shown more miracles than any messenger before, ever, for an entire decade. It’s done. They don’t need any more chances.

فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًۢا - prepare yourselves, brace yourselves for what’s coming as a result.
